analogy: Think of "Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion" through the chapter's own opening images: The family, caste, tribe and the market – these are the social institutions that have been considered in the last two chapters. These institutions were seen from the point of view of their role in forming communities and sustaining society. In this chapter we consider... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that the family, caste, tribe and the market – these are the social institutions that have been considered in the last two chapters.

The text explains that these institutions were seen from the point of view of their role in forming communities and sustaining society.

The text explains that in this chapter we consider an equally important aspect of such institutions, namely their role in creating and sustaining patterns of inequality and exclusion.

The text explains that for most of us who are born and live in India, social inequality and exclusion are facts of life.

The text explains that we see beggars in the streets and on railway platforms.

The text explains that we see young children labouring as domestic workers, construction helpers, cleaners and helpers in streetside restaurants (dhabas) and tea-shops.

The text explains that we are not surprised at the sight of small children, who work as domestic workers in middle class urban homes, carrying the school bags of older children to school.

The text explains that it does not immediately strike us as unjust that some children are denied schooling.

The text explains that some of us read about caste discrimination against children in schools; some of us face it.

The text explains that likewise, news reports about violence against women and prejudice against minority groups and the differently abled are part of our everyday lives.

The text explains that this everydayness of social inequality and exclusion often make them appear inevitable, almost natural.

The text explains that if we do sometimes recognise that inequality and exclusion are not inevitable, we often think of them as being ‘deserved’ or ‘justified’ in some sense.

The text explains that perhaps the poor and marginalised are where they are because they are lacking in ability, or haven’t tried hard enough to improve their situation?

The text explains that we thus tend to blame them for their own plight – if only they worked harder or were more intelligent, they wouldn’t be where they are.

The text explains that a closer examination will show that few work harder than those who are located at the lower ranks of society.

The text explains that as a South American proverb says – “If hard labour were really such a good thing, the rich would keep it all for themselves!” All over the world, back-breaking work like stone breaking, digging, carrying heavy weights, pulling rickshaws or carts is invariably done by the poor.

The text explains that s ociAl InequAlity In every society, some people have a greater share of valued resources – money, property, education, health, and power – than others.

The text explains that these social resources can be divided into three forms of capital – economic capital in the form of material assets and income; cultural capital such as educational qualifications and status; and social capital in the form of networks of contacts and social associations (Bourdieu 1986).

The text explains that patterns of unequal access to social resources are commonly called social inequality.

The text explains that this hierarchy then shapes people’s identity and experiences, their relations with others, as well as their access to resources and opportunities.

The text explains that social stratification is a society-wide system that unequally distributes social resources among categories of people.

The text explains that in more technologically advanced societies where people produce a surplus over and above their basic needs, however, social resources are unequally distributed to various social categories regardless of people’s innate individual abilities.

The text explains that it is closely linked to the family and to the inheritance of social resources from one generation to the next.

The text explains that often we discuss social exclusion and discrimination as though they pertain to differential economic resources alone.

The text explains that a prejudiced person’s preconceived views are often based on hearsay rather than on direct evidence, and are resistant to change even in the face of new information.

The text explains that in short, in a land with extensive natural resources, including diamonds and precious minerals, the majority of people lived in abject poverty.

The text explains that although other (i.e., ‘touchable’) low castes are also subjected to subordination and exploitation to some degree, they do not suffer the extreme forms of exclusion reserved for ‘untouchables.’ Dalits experience forms of exclusion that are unique and not practised against other groups – for instance, being prohibited from sharing drinking water sources or participating in collective religious worship, social ceremonies and festivals.

The text explains that the impoverished and exploited circumstances under which adivasis live can be traced historically to the pattern of accelerated resource extraction started by the colonial British government and continued by the government of independent India.

The text explains that the policy of capital-intensive industrialisation adopted by the Indian government required mineral resources and power-generation capacities which were concentrated in Adivasi areas.

The text explains that 84 Source: https://tribal.nic.in/downloads/Livelihood/Resource%20and%20Publications/tribalFaces.pdf Tribes of India 84 Indian Society

The text explains that were also a form of internal colonialism, subjugating adivasis and alienating the resources upon which they depended.

The text explains that projects such as the Sardar Sarovar dam on the river Narmada in Western India and the Polavaram dam on the river Godavari in Andhra Pradesh displaced hundreds of thousands of adivasis from their original habitats.

The text explains that many used both these resources to fight for women’s rights.

The text explains that while Ranade and Rammohun Roy belonged to one kind of nineteenth century upper caste and middle class social reformers, Jotiba Phule came from a socially excluded caste and his attack was directed against both caste and gender discrimination.

The text explains that each group can chose a topic relating to women’s r i g h t s o n w h i c h t h e y must collect information from newspapers, radio, television news or other source.

The text explains that you can draw from any of the innumerable regional sources of folklore, mythology, and traditional storytelling in India, or from any other part of the world.  Make a list of examples that show positive achievements of the disabled in different fields like music, art, etc.

The text explains that source: Anita Ghai ‘Disability in the Indian Context’, 2002:93 complexities of other categories like caste and tribe have received attention.

The text explains that 2002. ‘Marginalisation and Disability: experiences from the third world’, in Priestly, M. ed.

The text explains that disability and the Life Course: Global Perspectives.

The text explains that jeffery, Craig, Jeffery, Roger. and Jeffery, Patricia.

The text explains that 2005. ‘Broken Trajectories: Dalit Young Men and Formal Education’, in Chopra, Radhika. and Jeffery, Patricia. ed.

The text explains that disability Studies in India: Retrospect and Prospects.

The text explains that poverty by Social, Religious and Economic Groups in India and its Largest States: 1993–94 to 2011–12, Working Paper no.

The text explains that 2013–02, Programme on Indian Economic Policies, Columbia University.

The text explains that caste (Concepts in the Social Sciences Series).

The text explains that 1984. ‘A fight to Finish’, in Annual Report of the Indian National Congress 1939–1940.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 What Is S ocial about socIal and 5.2 Caste and tribe – systems Justifying and as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 What Is S ocial about socIal and 5.2 Caste and tribe – systems Justifying and to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
